speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Andrea Brasg. I'm interviewing Henia Bryer. Today's date is the 15th of November, 1995. The interview is being conducted in English. We are in Bloemfontein, South Africa. Tape 3. So can you tell me, what else happened in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I told you before, I lost all the members of my family. I lost contact with them, in any case. My father, as I mentioned before, was killed by the guards. He was beaten. My mother was sent to another camp. And my little sister was sent away with all the children. And one morning they were rounded up, and they were sent away to-- to Auschwitz, to the gas chambers. Now, if you remember in the film there was a scene where they were putting all the children onto that truck. But they concentrated, obviously, on the children that belonged to the people that worked for Schindler. The rest of the children were put onto that truck. And it was a-- a mad scene when-- when that truck left, because we were all standing on the-- on the square. Everybody was being counted. And the-- they had loudspeakers with German music playing lively tunes all the time. All the mothers were screaming and crying, and there the children were being led away in that-- in that truck. And by that time, we knew that they didn't stand a chance, that this was the last time we were-- we were going to see them. And my little sister was on one of-- was on that truck. They all went straight to Auschwitz, and they were all sent to the gas chambers. And after that, I was chosen to work for the SS women and-- as their maid. Now, that was also an experience. I-- they lived in-- in a house. There were five of them, I think. There was this-- the same one that was in-- in Majdanek, this Else Erlich. She was in charge. She was the officer. Then there was another one who was in charge of the-- of the women SS. Her name was Weber. And she was-- excuse me-- she was also in charge of me. She told me what to do. And there were another couple. She was an-- an older woman. She wasn't-- the others were all young. There were five of them living there in this house. And my job was to clean the whole house and do whatever they needed to be done. Now, I've never done this sort of work in my life. I didn't know where to start, and I didn't know where to-- to end. And-- and they probably were never used to having servants in the house, either, because they demanded the most impossible jobs. For example, they had there, like, shaggy carpets there. They-- they were made out of natural wool, I think sheepskins or something like that. And they insisted they must be washed every day. I mean, every person who knows would know that this is-- this is nonsense. But they insisted it's got to be washed every day. And in every room there was a-- a-- a carpet. And then there was always an enormous dish of dirty dishes that I had to wash in the morning. And the first few mornings, I just thought, this is-- they're going to shoot me, because I'll never be able to do all this work. And water was not available from the tap. You had to take it from a well, which was in a-- in a-- in a-- in the courtyard there, and that was outside of the camp. It wasn't in the camp. It was in a-- in the village, in Plaszow, where they requisitions the houses, and-- and they lived there. The-- the men officers lived there, the high officers only. And the-- and the women lived outside of the camp. The others lived inside the camp. They had houses there. So I had to walk with this woman every morning to the workplace, and then she accompanied me back to the gates of the camp every-- every afternoon. And in the morning, I'll face this pile of dirty dishes, and you had to pull the water from the well by hand, buckets and buckets of it. And I didn't know how to wash dishes. That was the true-- the truth. I didn't how to start. And I saw another-- a Ukrainian, an old Ukrainian soldier, who was working at the house next door. And I asked him please to show me what to do, because I didn't know what to do. And luckily, he was really very kind. He pulled the water up for me, and he showed me how to wash these dishes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I said to him, but I've got to wash all these floors and the carpets. How do I do that? So he said, you take a bucket of water, and you throw it over it. And you just spread it, and they will think it was washed. They won't know the difference. So thank god for this good advice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this old man was really very good to me. He pulled the water for me every morning. There was a-- a-- the water was already pulled when I got to-- to the well, because he-- he worked different hours, I think, because I hardly saw him since that first day. But he did leave water for me every morning. He filled that-- that whole thing with water. And I did as he said. I used to pull-- take a bucket of water, throw it on everything, and then just spread it around, and they thought it was all washed. This was the only way I could finish the work. Otherwise I could never have been able to-- to do that. And that was the routine.

speaker: interviewer
text: The advantages of it were that I had food. This-- this one woman that was in charge of me gave me food. I must say that. And-- and she explained to everybody-- she had to justify her-- her-- her kindness. So she used to tell them that I'm not really Jewish. I'm a mixed-- I'm mixed blood. I-- I'm not really Jewish, and that's why she's helping me. If they asked, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I worked there for all the time while I was in Plaszow, till-- till they took us-- till they sort of started also to liquidate the-- the camp and send everybody away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the other inmates feel about you working at the [? beginning? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were very happy that I used to-- whatever I could, I used to bring food back with me and share it with everybody. And at that stage, when I had no family, friendships were formed, and I must say the women helped each other tremendously. We-- we relied on each other, and the older women looked after the younger ones. And there was-- there was a lot of help. They-- they didn't-- on the contrary, they were-- they were pleased that at least I had food, and I could share some of it with-- with my friends. Now, everybody had to have some work, you know, to do something. And-- and this was really something that probably saved my life in the long run. Because if I didn't have this-- this sort of spare nourishment, I could never have survived Auschwitz and Bergen-Belsen. These few months where-- where-- when I had food helped a lot. I'm sure it saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was another thing there in-- in-- in Plaszow that I didn't mention. On a Sunday, we didn't work. And so they had all sorts of things for us, surprises. They used to always catch, and they used to amuse themselves, the-- the guards-- shoot people, kill people, hit people. It was the only day where we could wash the, you know, our clothes and get cleaned and clean the-- the barracks and so on. And they used to catch people for blood transfusions. They needed-- they needed blood for the wounded on the Russian Front. So they used to catch prisoners. They used to go into the barracks, whoever was there, haul them out, take a lot of blood. And with no nourishment, or really little of it, it was a-- it was a-- a death sentence. And one morning, I was in the barrack as well, and there they come. And they catch every-- and they-- and they choose a few. They-- they came to-- to take for blood. And we knew who they were because they were from the-- the medical-- they-- they wore this medical insignias. And we knew that when they came, they came to-- to take blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- I didn't know what to do. I was on my-- on my [? deck ?] there where I was resting, and I didn't know what to do. Because all the other women started to run, run. They tried to escape, but it was useless. There were two guards at the door. You couldn't escape. So I stayed were I was. I was on the-- on the bunk, and I didn't move. And this SS guard comes and he says, what's wrong with you? I didn't know what-- what to say. I said, I've got the typhus. And I said, I think I've got the typhus. So he takes my-- he puts his hand on my forehead and must have been hot from fright or whatever. And he says, [GERMAN], and he left me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went out to call the others, and-- and I was left there. So that was another lucky escape, I suppose. And I worked there for them all the time, until-- until they-- until they sent us away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from there, we were sent to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't remember much about the journey, because it's not a-- a long way from Plaszow to Auschwitz. It's-- it's quite near. But it was the usual procedure with the cattle trucks and with the-- piled into these-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of people and no food and no water. And we arrived in Auschwitz. It was the-- it was bleak. You come out of this railway-- this railway station, and it's just desolation greets you. And the usual selection straightaway. You got to strip and the selection. A lot of people were then sent to the left side. My best friend's mother was in front of me, and she was sent to the left side and a lot of other people. And we went through-- the receiving camp was Birkenau. They used to call it the Zigeunerlager, the-- the Gypsy camp. Conditions there were terrible. It was all that you saw in all the films after the liberation you saw in that Birkenau camp. But first we went to the-- to the bath, the usual bath, and stripped again and take all your clothes. And every one of us was given a bundle of clothes to put on. They also shaved all the women's heads. And-- and that group of a-- there was just a few they didn't shave. They-- they sort of checked for-- for lice and things, and I was one of the three or four that were not shaved. The others were all shaved. Their whole heads were shaved. They looked terrible. And we looked at that. All these girls, they looked terrible. And we were given bundles of clothes to put on. And I was given a bundle, and nothing fitted me. It was the most terrible thing. When I saw it, I thought, now this is it. I'll never be able to survive in this. I got-- for shoes, I got these wooden [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dutch shoes. You know those wooden things. And they didn't fit me. They were too small, and you can't stretch it. It's not leather or material. I couldn't get into them. And then I was given clothes of a-- of a small girl, a little girl, probably a child. So when I got dressed, I could hardly close that-- that-- that dress, and it was like a-- like a mini. And it wasn't warm enough. There was no coat. I was given a most unfortunate parcel of clothes. And I stood there in the courtyard, and I just cried. I didn't know what to do. I couldn't put the shoes on. I was holding in my-- in my hand these big, you know-- forgot what you call them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clogs, that's right. I couldn't put my foot into them, and I stood in the middle of that courtyard weeping bitterly. I didn't know what to do. And then a miracle happened. Somebody called my name over the-- the things. There was a-- a wire fence, and people on the other side were working with the clothes. They were probably men from our town, some of them, because somebody called my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, yes, who are you? So he says, never mind, just wait here. It was a young man on the other side. He recognized me, but I didn't know who he was. And I was crying so much I probably couldn't see who he was. And he says, just stay where you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came back five minutes later with a bundle of clothes for me. And it had a coat, a black-- I'll never forget that, because I was never so happy in my life-- a pair of shoes, stockings, and a black velvet coat and a-- and-- and a dress. Whatever it was, it-- it was clothes, and I could put them on, and-- and that was that. So from there, that was really something special. And then I went to-- then we were all taken to this Birkenau, this Zigeunerlager, and there we were sorted out. Another selection, again, and they chose from that whole group a-- again, a 100 young women and girls. And they-- and I met my mother there at Birkenau for half an hour. And-- and some of my friends were there. And my best friend, who is now in Israel, came with a-- with a-- with a-- a cup of soup for me. And she thought she was doing me such a big favor. But I came from Plaszow. I had food there. So I'd-- it wasn't-- she probably took away her-- her last ration from-- from her, and she brought it for me. And I met my mother there, but only very briefly. And they stayed in that camp, and I was chosen with the other 100, among the 100, to another camp in Auschwitz, which was called the Musterlager. Now, the Musterlager, it was there as an example. It was a-- it was like a showpiece for-- if, in the case, a-- a-- a Red Cross delegation would come. I never saw one, but they said they-- they used to come there before because they kept prisoners of wars and German prisoners there as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took the 100 of us, and we came to this big gate there with the-- with this 'Arbeit macht frei' sign, and we were put into barracks. These barracks were much better and cleaner than the ones in the other camp in Auschwitz. And we were put in there, and we stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the work was-- again, the standing from 5 o'clock in the morning, and it was starting to get cold. We-- it was starting to get cold, and it was-- all-- all I remember is the cold and the hunger. And we were in the street the whole day. The group that I was with, the 100, were divided in 10 again, and we were called Strassenkommando. It means we worked in the streets. So you were outdoors the whole day, from 5 o'clock in the morning till-- till the evening. And all we were given was a portion of soup during the day and then in the evening a slice of bread. And it was just terribly, terribly cold. We were-- we were-- we were carrying stones, that's all I remember, from one place to another. And there was no aim to it. There was no-- no purpose. We were just told to carry the stones from one place to another. But we did get to see the camp around. And I also forgot to mention that at the-- when we arrived in-- in Auschwitz, Mengele did the selection. He was there. And they used to call him the Angel of Death, the prisoners. And he did the-- the choosing, the-- the pointing to the left and to the right. This-- this-- we came in contact with a lot of strange people. There were German prisoners there as well. There were the Bibelforschers. They-- they had a different color of triangle. I think it was a lilac one. And there were the-- the murderers and-- and real, you know, prisoners that they-- instead of keeping them in prisons, they sent them in-- into the concentration camps. And one of them was our overseer, this kapo. He was-- he-- he was in charge of these different groups of 10. And [NON-ENGLISH], he was boasting how many people he killed before the war and while he was in there-- a real criminal. But he wasn't too bad to us, because he was in-- in prison, I suppose, a long time. And he was in charge of this group. But what we did-- I really don't know what we did there. We just carried stone, and we were outside. And all I remember is this terrible cold and the hunger. It was-- it was hard. And we walked endlessly, and we-- we were sort of told to carry these things from one place to another. And one day, we passed the block where they used to do the medical experiments. And I saw a-- a girl in the window that I knew, and I shouted to her. I-- I said to her, you're lucky you're indoors. So she shouted back, she said, don't be jealous. What they do to us here-- that's all she managed to say. She said, don't be jealous, and-- and that was that. And then later on, we found out about all these horrible experiments that they were doing on these prisoners. And well, that was the Auschwitz story. Nothing much changed. Just this long hours standing, being counted in the cold winter, and always the lack of food. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did get. That was done immediately after arrival. And do you want to see it?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: There it is. The prisoners did the numbers. And by the time we arrived there, they were so skilled, as you can see. It's-- it's very well done, that number. The letters are neat and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a-- with a needle. It wasn't electric or anything like that. They dipped it into ink, and they just pricked you with a needle. It was quite painful, but this was the least of our worries at that stage. If you've got it, can I put my arm down now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever celebrate any Jewish holidays or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not in Auschwitz, not in the camps. But I remember one incident. When we arrived in Auschwitz, we were told-- when we arrived in the second lager there-- we were told that it was Erev Yom Kippur. So we asked the-- the woman in charge to keep our rations for the next day, because we weren't going to eat on Yom Kippur. Well, predictably, they not only took the rations away, but they didn't give it to us the next day, either. And after that, we-- we didn't really know when the festivals were, and we never celebrated any.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes. I had my birthday in Auschwitz. I was 18 when I arrived in Auschwitz, and then in December I had my birthday. And oh, it was-- it was-- it was very, very emotional for me. But as I said before, friendships were formed, and I had a very good friend who looked after me. She was an older lady-- not older, she was probably 22 or 23 at the time. But in those days, a difference of a couple of years is a big difference. And she-- she went to work somewhere. I can't remember where. But she came back with a present for me-- an-- a-- a slice of bread. And it was the best gift I've ever had, or appreciated at least, on-- on that day in December. We stayed there till the beginning of January. And then they started to-- we were still there when they started to demolish the-- the gas chambers, but they didn't manage. They didn't have enough time. The Russians were approaching. And the Russians were in Auschwitz the beginning of January. I can't remember the date, but it was January 1944. Because they left all the old people and the-- and the feeble ones and the ones that couldn't move. And my mother was actually liberated by the Russians in Auschwitz, at that time. I didn't know it at the time, but she was left behind. And we went on this awful march from Auschwitz that was well-documented and described and written about, where we walked and walked and walked in the snow. And the road--- the snow was red, because on both sides of the road there were bodies. Whoever couldn't walk was shot on the spot. And we just, we-- I don't know how long it took, but more than, I think, about three days. At night, we-- we were told to just sit down for the night somewhere, and-- and the rest we walked. It's quite a long way from Auschwitz to Breslau. We walked till then. It was the most dreadful thing. You couldn't-- you couldn't walk. You wanted, actually, to drop everything. You didn't have anything, but we had some bread, which we found on the way. A-- a cart of bread was turned over, and so we each grabbed a loaf of bread. But we-- it was difficult to carry it. You-- you get so tired walking and-- and marching. And-- and it was-- but we held onto this bread. And I was practically carried by two other women, and it was-- it was terrible. It was dreadful. And the road-- I-- I just visualize it. I remember it like it was yesterday. All we heard was this crackling of-- of guns shooting, the dogs with the guards, people lying on both sides of the road. And us just going along.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to march at the same pace?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and if you stayed behind, you were shot. You see, the guards were changing, but we didn't change. They had relief. They-- every couple of hours, others came with-- with-- with dogs, and others took over and-- and but we didn't have a break. So that was the terrible march to Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people marched?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was a long, long column. And by the time we got there, I suppose half of them were probably killed on the way. I don't know if anybody escaped during that march. I have no idea. Of course, it-- it was you-- you just trapped up in yourself in a situation like that, and you just tried to stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the weather like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was winter. It was snowing. It was cold. It was January, and it was cold. It was snow all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you arrived--

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived there, and they took us to the railway-- not a platform. It was just outside of town probably. And they loaded us into these cattle trucks. But these were trucks with a difference. They were open. They had no roof. And we were packed on them like sardines. There was only standing place in them. And from there, we were taken in those trucks to Bergen-Belsen. And if you look at the map, it's right across Europe. It's the other side of Europe, right to the west side. Bergen-Belsen is near Hamburg, between Hamburg and Hanover. It's a camp. I don't know how long we-- we were in those trucks, but a lot of people died on the way. It was snowing. It was cold. There was no place to rest, of course, there was only standing. So you-- you stood among the dead people as well as the living. And it took a long time. It seemed like a-- like an eternity. But it must have taken a good couple of days before we got to-- to Bergen-Belsen. And we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was liberated in Auschwitz. I didn't know it at the time, but she's remained. I told you that only the-- the older people and-- and the ones-- she was very ill. She actually was sick, and she couldn't move. She-- you know, in those days I thought she was old, but she wasn't. She was still in-- probably in her 40s or early 50s. But she couldn't go on the march. She was very ill. So she was left in the barrack, and they didn't have time to kill all the people. So she was liberated by the Russians, and they took very good cares of her. And we met later on, but that we-- we'll come to that later. We arrived in-- in Bergen, the station there, in the middle of the night. We're taken off from the-- well, we're told to jump from the-- these trucks, these wagons, and we had to still walk a long way to the camp. We walked through the forest. It was pitch dark. It was snowing. The forest was beautiful, covered with snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're just thinking-- we saw lights from houses, from homes far away-- and one couldn't help thinking, god, these people are lucky. They-- what-- what-- what did I do to deserve this? Why am I here, and lots of people are in their homes and warm and comfortable with food, with everything? And we are marching here in a forest, and don't know where we're going and what-- what awaits at the end of this. Did those people know what was going on?
